texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The scarcity of freshwater and availability of solar radiation in many isolated regions constitute the perfect condition for applying different solar desalination technologies like solar membrane distillation (SMD) to supply the shortfalls. Nowadays, renewable energy driven desalination technologies are considered suitable for decentralized systems where water demand is lower than 20 10 3 l per day, being the recommended human basic requirements for drinking, hygiene, sanitation services and food preparation in the range of 50-80 l per day and inhabitant [1].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A solar desalination system based on membrane distillation (MD) is presented and evaluated. In the context of a European project, the MEDESOL project, a pilot plant was built to evaluate the system, which consists of three commercial MD modules coupled with a static solar collector's field. The MD modules employed have been developed and manufactured by the Swedish company Scarab AB. They have a flat sheet air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) configuration with a total membrane surface area per module of 2.8 m 2 . The MD system is intended to be technically simple to operate, robust and able to cover water demands of small settlements. It also contemplates the use of a multi-stage layout to minimize energy consumption.
texts.text: A solar desalination system based on membrane distillation (MD) is presented and evaluated. In the context of a European project, the MEDESOL project, a pilot plant was built to evaluate the system, which consists of three commercial MD modules coupled with a static solar collector's field. The MD modules employed have been developed and manufactured by the Swedish company Scarab AB. They have a flat sheet air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) configuration with a total membrane surface area per module of 2.8 m 2 . The MD system is intended to be technically simple to operate, robust and able to cover water demands of small settlements. It also contemplates the use of a multi-stage layout to minimize energy consumption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experiments were run during solar hours (the layout didn't include heat storage) and addressed to characterize the performance of the system (i.e. distillate production and quality, thermal efficiency and recovery ratio) as a function of operation variables and salt concentration, as well as to identify the operating capacities and the potential improvements of the MD technology. Aqueous NaCl solutions of 1 and 35 g/l concentration were used as feed. Temperatures up to 85 ◦ C in the feed and up to 75 ◦ C in the refrigeration were employed. Maximum specific distillate flux values registered were in the range of 7 l/h m 2 . Multi-stage layouts were tested in order to evaluate the improvement of the system's thermal efficiency and recovery ratio. The MD technology assessed proved to be suitable for coupling with transient solar thermal energy but inefficiencies inherent to scaling-up compared to laboratory experiences reported in literature were also identified, namely affecting specific distillate production and thermal consumption. The results of the characterization, performance assessment and operational issues description of the pilot plant are shown.

Solar energy is being regarded as one of the possible solutions to tackle this energy problem as is considered cheap and accessible and especially suitable in these particular areas [4]. When looking for potential desalination processes that can be appropriate for these areas, some features are more than desirable. The chosen process must be robust in order to stand both hard climate conditions and varying conditions of raw water, should have advantageous attributes regarding the coupling with solar energy (bear unstable operation conditions) and should constitute stand-alone operating systems (practically maintenance-free).

Solar energy is being regarded as one of the possible solutions to tackle this energy problem as is considered cheap and accessible and especially suitable in these particular areas [4]. When looking for potential desalination processes that can be appropriate for these areas, some features are more than desirable. The chosen process must be robust in order to stand both hard climate conditions and varying conditions of raw water, should have advantageous attributes regarding the coupling with solar energy (bear unstable operation conditions) and should constitute stand-alone operating systems (practically maintenance-free).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So far, the specific solutions given to tackle this casuistry have been basically two, photovoltaics coupled with reverse osmosis systems (PV-RO) and solar thermally driven distillation systems (STD). Although PV and RO are mature technologies and the most widespread combination used when coupling renewals with desalination [2], with commercial application in many cases [5], there are still some difficulties when operating small scale PVdriven standalone RO systems [6] like the dependence of the chemical pre-treatment on raw water quality and the difficulty to manage it. Moreover, RO operation entails significant automation and has additional needs like accessibility to spare parts and skilled workers [7] which are against the requirements previously stated. Besides, RO systems have the requirements of a continuous process [8] but solar energy is intrinsically discontinuous. Amongst thermally driven systems for small capacities, solar stills are the simplest option because of their practically non-existing operational and maintenance needs and their simplicity of construction. But their main drawbacks are a low yield (the production capacity of a simple type still is in the range of 2-5 l/m 2 per day [9]) and a very low thermal efficiency that results in large specific collector area per cubic meter of distillated water. Their operation is also simple but it is counterbalanced by the problems that arise in the long term, like dust deposition and algae and scale formation, which make the efficiency to be even lower [2].

texts.orig: SMD technology is intended to reach better energy efficiencies than other solar desalination processes while keeping an easy and robust operation and potentially low operational costs [11]. MD has been developed since late 60s, but it wasn't until the early 80s, with the improved design of the modules and the development of new and cheaper membranes, that the scientific community became interested in the technology. Recent approaches of coupling MD with low-grade energy have boosted the interest on this technology [6,12-17].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Membrane distillation
texts.text: 2. Membrane distillation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD is a thermal separation process, in which only water vapor or other volatile molecules are transported through a hydrophobic porous membrane. When a temperature difference is created between both sides of the membrane, a vapor partial pressure difference appears, constituting the driving force of the process. This causes the vapor to evaporate from the surface of the liquid/vapor interface of the hot side, pass through the porous membrane and condensate on the colder side. The solely role of the membrane, is to hold the vapor/liquid interface that is created on both sides of it as its hydrophobic nature does not let the water molecules nor the solute ones to pass across it [18-20]. The membrane keeps its hydrophobicity as long as the Liquid Entry Pressure (LEP) is not overcome. This LEP depends on both the membrane characteristics and the surface tension of the solution as it is described in the Cantor-Laplace equation [20]. The desired characteristics of the membranes for MD are basically: a low resistance to mass transfer to help vapor flux, a high LEP to prevent it from wetting and a low thermal conductivity to minimize heat conduction losses and keep the necessary temperature gradient between both sides of it [19,21]. The membranes currently used for MD are designed for MF applications and the operational performances of these membranes are somehow limited for MD [19,22]. There are still few experiences in designing specific membranes for MD. Recent laboratory experiments reached specific fluxes up to 80 kg/h m 2 with enhanced direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) [10]. These results are promising if they are compared with RO fluxes (between 25 and 40 kg/h m 2 , normalized values for nominal conditions) [23,24].

texts.orig: MD has been mostly used in industry to concentrate aqueous solutions of thermally sensitive products like fruit juices, chemicals and antibiotics [25] and also has been employed for wastewater and desalination processes [26,27]. texts.text: MD has been mostly used in industry to concentrate aqueous solutions of thermally sensitive products like fruit juices, chemicals and antibiotics [25] and also has been employed for wastewater and desalination processes [26,27]. The use of MD for desalination is supported by some advantages:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It can reach a theoretical 100% rejection of the ions, macromolecules, colloids, microorganisms and other non-volatile particles.
texts.text: It can reach a theoretical 100% rejection of the ions, macromolecules, colloids, microorganisms and other non-volatile particles.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Its performance is theoretically not affected by salt concentration.
texts.text: Its performance is theoretically not affected by salt concentration.

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: There is a minimum chemical interaction between membranes and feed solutions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It uses lower temperatures and pressures than other thermal and membrane desalination processes.

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Even though MD is a promising technology, a small number of practical experiences have been developed in recent years and very few studies dealing with key topics like specific energy consumption, operational issues or long-term performance of MD pilot plants are reported in the literature. Further experimentation can help to overcome the shortcomings that jeopardize its commercial implementation, like its still high thermal energy consumption and the general lack of specific membrane and modules designed for MD purposes.

texts.orig: of minimum maintenance and easy to handle desalination technology based on SMD. As it was aforementioned, MD unlike other membrane technologies can be easily coupled with solar energy due to its compatibility with the transient nature of the energy. The system to be developed was intended to supply isolated rural areas with water difficulties. The projected design involved the concept of multistage MD in order to minimize the energy requirements, the development of a specific static solar collector (a Compound Parabolic Collector, CPC type) to be energy-efficient at MD working temperatures and the use of advanced non-fouling coatings for heat exchangers to avoid scaling at such temperatures. This work was done in the course of the first stage of the project, during which the MD prototype, which consisted of three commercial AGMD modules manufactured by Scarab AB, was coupled with a CPC solar field at Plataforma Solar de Almería (PSA) facilities to be tested with saline solutions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Experimental set-up
texts.text: 4. Experimental set-up

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The test facility was located in Plataforma Solar de Almería (PSA), Spain, and consisted of two independent hydraulic loops connected through a heat exchanger (10 plates titanium heat exchanger by HRS Spiratube) (see Fig. 1). The solar loop supplied the thermal energy and operated with RO treated tap water in order to protect solar collectors from corrosion and scaling. The desalination loop operated with a marine salt aqueous solution of varying concentration and was in turn divided into two independent circuits: the cold and the hot one, respectively.
texts.text: The test facility was located in Plataforma Solar de Almería (PSA), Spain, and consisted of two independent hydraulic loops connected through a heat exchanger (10 plates titanium heat exchanger by HRS Spiratube) (see Fig. 1). The solar loop supplied the thermal energy and operated with RO treated tap water in order to protect solar collectors from corrosion and scaling. The desalination loop operated with a marine salt aqueous solution of varying concentration and was in turn divided into two independent circuits: the cold and the hot one, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solar loop was designed and installed for a former project (AQUASOL project: development of an advanced hybrid solargas multi-effect distillation system). It was composed of 252 stationary solar collectors (CPC 3E+ by Ao Sol Energias Renováveis, S.A.) arranged in four rows of 63 collectors (35 ◦ tilted and East-West orientated). It had a total area of approximately 500 m 2 and a 24 m 3 thermal storage system based on water [28]. The solar field was designed to supply a MED desalination plant with a 3 m 3 /h capacity and therefore it was over dimensioned for the MD-prototype energy requirements. However, the power and the temperature supplied to the desalination system were regulated by controlling both the solar field and the heat exchanger water flow rates (by means of a by-pass circuit).
texts.text: The solar loop was designed and installed for a former project (AQUASOL project: development of an advanced hybrid solargas multi-effect distillation system). It was composed of 252 stationary solar collectors (CPC 3E+ by Ao Sol Energias Renováveis, S.A.) arranged in four rows of 63 collectors (35 ◦ tilted and East-West orientated). It had a total area of approximately 500 m 2 and a 24 m 3 thermal storage system based on water [28]. The solar field was designed to supply a MED desalination plant with a 3 m 3 /h capacity and therefore it was over dimensioned for the MD-prototype energy requirements. However, the power and the temperature supplied to the desalination system were regulated by controlling both the solar field and the heat exchanger water flow rates (by means of a by-pass circuit).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The desalination loop consisted of two independent 2 m 3 fiber glass reinforced polypropylene tanks used as hot and cold water reservoirs and their respective 2 kWe centrifugal stainless steel pumps. Two marine salt water solutions of 1 and 35 g/l (TDS of standard seawater) were employed in the experiments as feed solutions. Feed solution was heated up passing through the heat exchanger and pumped into the MD unit/s where the desalination process took place. Likewise, deionised water was used as refrigerant and was cooled down if necessary using an air cooler (by BTU). After the MD process, both cold and hot water streams returned to their corresponding tanks closing both circuits while distillate was discarded. All the experimental set-up was monitored with specific instrumentation for the mass and energy balances (see Fig. 1). Conductivity transmitters (LRD 01-7 with controller LF29 by WTW); electromagnetic flow meters (Proline Promag 50P by E + H), a paddle-wheel flow meter for the distillate (8031 by Bürket), pressure transmitters (Cerabar PMP41 relative by E + H and S10 absolute by WIKA) and threaded resistance thermometers equipped with thermowell (TR10-C by WIKA) were employed. Pressure values were specially controlled at the entrance of the MD units to avoid values higher than 0.2 bar (gauge), which is the maximum allowed pressure to prevent from membrane wetting. Finally, a pyranometer (CM6B by Kipp + Zonen) was used to measure the global solar radiation on titled angle (35 ◦ same as collector tilt). A SCADA system was implemented to record all the measured variables.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flat sheet AGMD modules installed at the facility were manufactured by the Swedish company Scarab Development AB (see Fig. 2). They consist of 10 plastic cassettes each, with a total membrane area of 2.8 m 2 . The cassettes are injected molded plastic frames that contain two parallel flat sheet membranes, feed and exit channels for the hot water and two condensing walls. The channels for the cooling water are formed between the condensing walls of adjacent cassettes by stacking them together; as a result varying module sizes can be assembled. The membrane itself is made of spun-bounded PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene) with a porosity of 80%, 0.2 mm of thickness, an average pore size of 0.2 𝛍 m and an air gap width of 1 mm. The module's dimensions are 63 cm width, 73 cm high with a stack thickness of 17.5 cm.

texts.orig: during the sunlight hours (around 8 hours). After the operation process, the system was stopped and the whole circuit was emptied. Although the system worked in closed-circuit mode, the distillate was discarded and therefore salt concentration increased throughout the experiment (around 1-2 mS/cm higher depending on the production) and consequently, it had to be restored with fresh water for the next operation day. The operational conditions were intended to stay as stable as possible. Measurements were taken after steady state conditions were reached and in order of increasing temperature and flow rate to avoid possible thermal inertia. However, hot side temperature could only be approximately controlled because of the inherent fluctuation of the solar energy and the absence of a thermal storage system in the facility. The outlet temperature of the solar field varied accordingly to the fluctuation of the radiation throughout the day. As both loops (solar and desalination) were connected by means of a heat exchanger, the final inlet temperature to the desalination loop could only be maintained constant between a certain range of variation. The temperature could vary a maximum of ± 2 ◦ C and data that fell out of that interval were discarded. Temperature was maintained within that range for at least half an hour/an hour (depending on the operation conditions), and data were then collected.

texts.orig: The scaled and centered regression coefficients based on MLR analysis in Eq. (1) show the influence of each of the variables on the response (distillate yield). The variables that show a higher contribution to the distillate production are in this order: T hot , F hot and their interaction. These results support previous observations [19,29-32]. The cold side variables, especially F cold show a much lower impact on distillate yield. The lower influence of T cold and F cold on the production of distillate compared to that of the hot feed parameters on MD has been also reported by many authors [31,32]. These lower effects are especially negligible in the case of the AGMD, because in this configuration the heat transfer coefficient in the air gap dominates the overall heat transfer coefficient and therefore changes on the coolant temperature and flow rate will have theoretically little effects on the AGMD distillate flux [19,32,33]. Experimental results show a maximum rate production of 20.2 l/h per module, which corresponds to the maximum T hot , F hot and F cold (i.e. 96.0 ◦ C; 20.0 l/min) and the minimum T cold (i.e. 25.0 ◦ C).

texts.orig: predicted by Antoine's equation. Therefore, a higher temperature should imply exponentially higher distillate flow rates and so has been reported by many authors [32,35,36]. However, experimental data in this case showed a linear behaviour. Models also predict that MD has potential thermal inefficiencies that reduce the available amount of heat to be used for distillate production. In the case of the simplest MD configuration, DCMD, heat conduction across the membrane is the main source of inefficiency and distillate production is limited by the heat transfer to the membrane surface. The AGMD configuration interposes an air gap with a lower thermal transfer coefficient to minimize these heat conduction losses but creates an additional mass transfer resistance that dominates the process which is the air gap itself.

texts.orig: Processes that show a linear response to delta T (in this case, between cold and hot sides of the membrane) and do not depend on hot side temperature are limited by heat transfer, therefore if the system is limited by heat transfer, distillate flux should follow this behaviour (increase linearly with delta T and be independent of hot side temperature). When the distillate production of the evaluated MD module is represented as a function of the delta T for different ranges of hot feed temperature (see Fig. 4), a linear response appears and no appreciable differences can be found between different hot feed temperature ranges; i.e. 80 s (90 ◦ C > T hot > = 80 ◦ C), 70 s (80 ◦ C > T hot > = 70 ◦ C) and 60 s (70 ◦ C > T hot > = 60 ◦ C). The observed behaviour of the modules in these experiments could therefore correspond to that of a DCMD system in which conduction is the main source of inefficiencies and heat transfer to the membrane surface is the limiting process but certainly not to that of a AGMD system, in which diffusional mass transport of water vapour across the air gap is expected to dominate the distillate flux.

texts.orig: 6.1.1. Leakage problems
texts.text: 6.1.1. Leakage problems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High distillate conductivity values, around 400-500 𝛍 S/cm (for 1500-2000 𝛍 S/cm of feed conductivity) were observed from the very beginning of the experimentation. This was a result of leakage which was identified after 1 week of experimental campaign and had to be subtracted from the total distillate flow rate in order to obtain real data on distillate production (i.e., percentage of leak around 10-30%, depending on the distillate production). Membrane wetting is a weak point of MD. Membranes used for MD purposes should be porous, thin, have a small pore size (in the order of 𝛍 m), a high surface tension of the feed solution in contact with the membrane and a low surface energy. The membrane used in this study theoretically meets the above mentioned properties, consequently the leakage should be caused by either a pressure spike that made the membranes lose part of their hydrophobicity [37,38] or, as reported by Cheng and Wiersma in 1983 [39], by crystal growth of salt through the membrane during shutdown periods that would attack the membrane hydrophobicity and cause water clogging of some pores, leading to distillate quality affection. After the leakage was detected, modules were drained and dried by means of a sweeping air stream for a few days, but conductivity values did not improve. Structural damage of the modules was then considered. MD units were opened and membranes were examined. No evidence of membrane damage or scaling was found but gaskets and O-rings were misshapen and consequently replaced. As a result, conductivity values of the distillate were reduced more than significantly (<10 𝛍 S/cm, for 1500-2000 𝛍 S/cm of feed conductivity). However, membrane hydrophobicity seemed to be affected and conductivity values increased progressively from average values of 40-80 𝛍 S/cm (for 48-50 mS/cm of feed conductivity) right after the refurbishment, to values ranging 1-6 mS/cm (also for 48-50 mS/cm of feed conductivity) during the last set of experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The same factorial design as before was intended to be applied for the experiments with the 35 g/l (conductivity of 48-50 mS/cm) aqueous salt solution. However, a significant raise in the conductivity of the distillate when working with low flow rates (i.e. 5.0 and 12.5 l/min and their combination) was detected, and regarding the previous leakage problems it was decided to evaluate the performance of the MD module as a function of the temperature and working only at the nominal flow rate (i.e. F hot and F cold = 20.0 l/min) to guarantee the integrity of the membranes. Experimental results showed an average decrease of 14% in distillate production when the 35 g/l marine water salt solution was used as feed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This effect is commonly explained by the salinity influence in the water vapour pressure of a system limited by vapour diffusion [32,37,40]. But this affection is very small unless very high concentrate brines are used, i.e.: a reduction of the 16% in distillate production increasing from 20 g/l to 50 g/l was reported by Alklaibi and Lior in 2006 [35], and can only lead to very small flux declines. From the point of view of a system limited by heat transfer, higher feed water salinity would require higher temperature drops across the membrane to initiate distillate flux; this would lead to greater conduction heat losses and lower available heat input for water evaporation and therefore a lower distillate flux. Presumably, a combination of both effects is occurring. Nevertheless, this salt influence on distillate production had an effect on the rest of the performance parameters evaluated. However, the main influence of the feed salt concentration, in the case of our system, is not on the distillate production but on the quality of the distillate.

On the one hand, inefficiencies associated to up-scaling seem to be affecting. A bigger membrane surface area associated with up-scaling implies higher heat losses to the surroundings. Other likeable effects of the upscaling are the pressure losses and a bad distribution of the flow inside the module. On the other hand, the fact that the MD process is a non-isothermal process and that in a solar driven process the operation conditions under which the system is operated are unstable due to the variable solar heat input mean that the system does never operate under steady state conditions, which is also a continuous source of inefficiencies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3. Distillate quality
texts.text: 6.3. Distillate quality

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The quality of the distillate was evaluated by means of its conductivity. Distillate conductivity values are represented in Fig. 6 as a function of delta T for both feed salt water solutions (i.e. 1 and 35 g/l). Conductivity values improve with greater delta T . Data show a remarkable worsening in distillate conductivity when the 35 g/l salt water solution was used as feed (same deterioration behaviour was reported by Banat and Simandl [32]). Values not larger than 12 𝛍 S/cm were registered when working with the 1 g/l feed solution but, an average value of 40-60 𝛍 S/cm (and never below 20 𝛍 S/cm) was measured when working with the 35 g/l feed solution. The greatest conductivity values were observed at the beginning of each experiment but improved rapidly with distillate production within the first hours. The fact that the operation was not continuous (see experimental procedure) could explain those high initial values. When the membrane falls dry and especially after working at high temperature, crystal growth of salt through the membrane can occur, causing water clogging of some pores, leading distillate quality to worsen. Continuous evaporation prevents the membrane from wetting, therefore better distillate conductivity values would be expected with uninterrupted operation of the system.

texts.text: 6.4. Thermal efficiency of the MD module

texts.orig: The parameter chosen to evaluate the thermal performance of the module was the performance ratio (PR) a generally used parameter amongst thermal processes and specifically amongst thermal desalination processes. In SI units, the PR indicates the amount of distillate (in kg) produced per each 2326 kJ of thermal energy supplied to the process, 2326 kJ is the energy needed to evaporate 1 kg of water at 1 bar of pressure, so a PR = 1 means a thermal efficiency equal to a simple one-effect distillation process. texts.text: The parameter chosen to evaluate the thermal performance of the module was the performance ratio (PR) a generally used parameter amongst thermal processes and specifically amongst thermal desalination processes. In SI units, the PR indicates the amount of distillate (in kg) produced per each 2326 kJ of thermal energy supplied to the process, 2326 kJ is the energy needed to evaporate 1 kg of water at 1 bar of pressure, so a PR = 1 means a thermal efficiency equal to a simple one-effect distillation process. The performance ratio is calculated as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: PR = /NAK · /SUBw ( T dist , P atm) · F dist Q in + Q dist (2)

texts.label: text

To calculate Q in and Q dist the following energy balances was applied:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Values obtained for PR were ranging between 0.28 and 0.79 depending on the delta T applied. PR values can be translated into specific thermal energy consumption (STEC), which is the thermal energy consumed per volume unit of distillate produced. The above range of PR values corresponds to STEC values between 2200 and 810 kWht/m 3 respectively. Results confirm a linear response to the delta T and a much lesser influence of the hot side absolute temperature (see Fig. 7). Experimental results also showed a 8-16% decrease in PR values when the 35 g/l marine salt solution was used as feed, corresponding to an extra energy supply in the range of 100-400 kWht/m 3 of distillate produced. In general, PR values are greater when greater delta T is used (regardless of the hot feed temperature applied). This can be explained by the fact that PR, as calculated in Eq. (2), depends on both distillate production and thermal power input. In the case of this MD system and the experiments held to evaluate the PR, Q in did not experimented a remarkable fluctuation, conversely the distillate production did. Therefore, the variations of PR with salinity reflect those of the actual distillate production between both salt concentration feeds: the greater the delta T the better the PR reached values are (see Figs. 5 and 7). The less significant differences in PR observed at lower delta T are also in consonance with the possibility discussed before of a continuous leakage taking place through the membrane, which in absence of enough thermal driving force (low delta T ) would mask the distillation process. The immediate outcome of these results is that the system can take advantage of the practically no importance of the hot absolute temperature and work at high delta T and lower feed temperatures with almost no effect in the thermal performance or in the distillate production. Therefore, the use of low grade heat sources such as solar is more than justified in this case of MD. Nev-

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Multistage concept
texts.text: 7. Multistage concept

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The set of experiments conducted to evaluate the multi-stage concept could not be held until all the MD modules were refurbished, which happened close to the conclusion of the project. As explained before, the MD module used was not specifically designed to internally recover the latent heat of evaporation. The design of the module (i.e. flat sheet membranes) and the distribution of the flow inside the module may not be suitable to allow the transfer of latent heat of distillate condensation to the cold stream used as refrigerant. The multistage concept is a widely used configuration (i.e. Multi Stage Flash Distillation, Multi Effect Distillation) consisting of several stages, in this case several MD modules, arranged in series to externally recover part of the heat input. To assess the multistage concept three different configurations were evaluated: 1, 2 and 3 MD modules connected in series and counter current flow to maximize delta T in every module. Consequently, the hydraulic circuit of the plant had to be modified (see Fig. 8). There was only one closed circuit and therefore only one tank was used as reservoir. Cold water was pumped from the tank, cooled down by means of the air cooler and passed through the modules where was gradually warmed by the latent heat of condensation and the conduction heat transfer from the hot side of the membrane. At the end of the cold circuit additional thermal energy was supplied by the solar field until the desired hot inlet temperature was reached and the stream was then used as hot feed. Brine was pumped back to the tank and distillate was collected, monitored and discarded.

texts.orig: The recovery ratio (RR) was calculated as the ratio (percentage) of distillate yield to total feed. Results are shown in Fig. 10. Again, figures got better with the multistage concept but were never greater than 2%. RR was considerably improved in the case of the 3MD configuration with respect to the other two (by a factor up to 2.24). No appreciable difference could be found between the results for 1 and 2 modules unless a delta T higher than 40 ◦ C was applied, in which case the improvement was up to a factor of 1.34. Regarding the results working with 3 MD modules in series, experimental data also showed that in this case PRH was more or less irrespective of the delta T applied and no further improvement or even a slight worsening was observed when a delta T beyond 40 ◦ C was used. A closer look to data pointed out that distillate production/RR remained constant (see Fig. 10) and distillate and condenser outlet temperatures increased; this can explain the lower PRH values for 3MD configuration obtained beyond this point as depicted in Fig. 9.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8. Conclusions
texts.text: 8. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An evaluation of the AGMD Scarab modules for desalination in a pilot plant coupled with a static solar collector field as sole thermal input was carried out. Special attention was devoted to the thermal efficiency of the system and its improvement by means of the multistage concept.
texts.text: An evaluation of the AGMD Scarab modules for desalination in a pilot plant coupled with a static solar collector field as sole thermal input was carried out. Special attention was devoted to the thermal efficiency of the system and its improvement by means of the multistage concept.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The modules showed specific distillate flux values up to a maximum of 6.5 l/h m 2 (delta T of 65 ◦ C and 1 g/l salt solution as feed), a productivity decay around 14% with increasing salinity (from 1 to 35 g/l) and a linear flux increase with the delta T across the membrane, with only a slight dependence on the hot side absolute temperature. The two latter facts point out that for this MD technology, distillate production seems to be not limited by vapour diffusion, as expected from an AGMD system but by the transfer of heat, namely the heat transfer to the membrane surface as would be the case of a DCMD system. To solve this matter, computational calculations should be performed to evaluate the internal fluid dynamics of the module.
texts.text: The modules showed specific distillate flux values up to a maximum of 6.5 l/h m 2 (delta T of 65 ◦ C and 1 g/l salt solution as feed), a productivity decay around 14% with increasing salinity (from 1 to 35 g/l) and a linear flux increase with the delta T across the membrane, with only a slight dependence on the hot side absolute temperature. The two latter facts point out that for this MD technology, distillate production seems to be not limited by vapour diffusion, as expected from an AGMD system but by the transfer of heat, namely the heat transfer to the membrane surface as would be the case of a DCMD system. To solve this matter, computational calculations should be performed to evaluate the internal fluid dynamics of the module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Leakage problems rose from the very beginning of the experimentation. Firstly, structural problems seemed to be the main cause. After module refurbishment, increasing conductivity values were registered in the long-term operation due to suspected membrane damage (i.e. membrane wetting). Actual causes for membrane wetting in the long term are unknown (modules were not opened after the last set of experiments) but it could be due to a combination of membrane crystal flushing, scaling and fibre rupture. However, structural module problems (loose bolts, misshapen gaskets, etc.) cannot be discarded.

After module refurbishment, increasing conductivity values were registered in the long-term operation due to suspected membrane damage (i.e. membrane wetting). Actual causes for membrane wetting in the long term are unknown (modules were not opened after the last set of experiments) but it could be due to a combination of membrane crystal flushing, scaling and fibre rupture. However, structural module problems (loose bolts, misshapen gaskets, etc.) cannot be discarded.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane wetting is one of the most crucial problems to be solved in MD long-term operation. Apart from the leakage problems faced during the experimentation period, distillate conductivity got higher (i.e. from values <10 𝛍 S/cm to average values of 40-60 𝛍 S/cm) with increasing salinity and lower delta T . Although conductivity values are expected to be improved with continuous operation, such low salt rejection values seemed to indicate that the membrane used was not the most adequate for desalination of sea water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The best thermal efficiency value registered, evaluated by means of the classical PR, was 0.79 which corresponds to a specific thermal energy consumption of 810 kWht/m 3 . A range of decrease between 8 and 16% in PR values (extra energy supply in the range of 100-400 kWht/m 3 ) was also registered when increasing the salinity of the feed from 1 to 35 g/l.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A multistage concept for this MD technology could reduce the heat consumption noticeably (up to 55% of thermal energy input reduction) by recovering the latent heat of condensation and also the heat transferred by conduction on the cold stream and using it to preheat the feed. However, specific thermal energy consumption of the process is still very high and the energy stored in the brine is greater than the energy accumulated in the refrigeration stream. An ideal MD system should be able to maximize the energy transferred by latent heat and minimize the amount that comes from conduction. In the case of this MD module, multi-stage concept is an appropriate option to recover part of the heat through the cold stream. Experimental results indicate that PRH improves with the number of stages connected in series but also seem to point out that only to some extent. This point could not be further investigated because of the evenly worsening of the MD modules leakage problems which, at the final part of the experimental campaign, distorted the experimental results.

This point could not be further investigated because of the evenly worsening of the MD modules leakage problems which, at the final part of the experimental campaign, distorted the experimental results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Specific fluxes, salt concentration influence on performance and thermal efficiency showed much worse results than the ones that can be found in the literature for laboratory scale experiences. Although Scarab MD technology proved to be suitable for coupling with solar energy, thermal efficiency and salt rejection are the key factors to be improved in the case of the application of this technology to seawater desalination. The more efficient the technology is, the smaller solar collector's area is needed and therefore the technology can become competitive with PV-RO or HumidificationDehumidification, especially since MD operation is cheaper and less demanding.
